# What if the White House correspondents had been in Mali?

**From:** The Cosmopolitan Globalist <claireberlinski+critical-conditions@substack.com>
**Date:** Tue, 28 Apr 2026 17:06:33 +0000
**Source:** [View original](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9jbGFpcmViZXJsaW5za2kuc3Vic3RhY2suY29tL3Avd2hhdC1pZi10aGUtd2hpdGUtaG91c2UtY29ycmVzcG9uZGVudHM_.-vj-lzD6gL7M08cLjyNx5Vs6x8bPLZx9qc3TasQI4PM?)

---

View this post on the web at https://claireberlinski.substack.com/p/what-if-the-white-house-correspondents

Dan and I discussed the strange event at the White House Correspondents’ dinner, and I lamented that all those journalists were in Washington instead of all the places around the world that could really use more attention. 
From Dan’s show notes:   
We began with the latest failed seeming attempted assassination of Trump. No one was hurt, the system functioned, and yet the normalization of such episodes says something profound about the political climate in the United States. As we argued, isolated acts of violence don’t necessarily prove anything on their own, but the broader ecosystem—marked by polarization, saturation of firearms, and a kind of societal nihilism—cannot be ignored. The real debate, as usual, wasn’t about the event, but about whether anything meaningful can be done in response.
From there, we pivoted to what isn’t being covered: Mali, and the steady, underreported advance of jihadist groups across West Africa. Claire made the crucial point that al-Qaeda’s growing sophistication, including drone capabilities, should be front-page news. It isn’t. Why? Because non-African audiences don’t click on Africa, and media organizations follow the clicks. Having once overseen AP coverage across the region, I can attest: This is not a new problem, but it’s becoming a more dangerous one.
We also touched on France’s complicated role in Africa, the West’s intermittent attention span, and the enduring question of responsibility—whether for Libya’s destabilization or the broader failure to stem extremist movements.
As always, the conversation ranged—from Trump’s foreign policy theatrics to King Charles’ hypothetical moral backbone—but the underlying theme was constant: a world where spectacle dominates attention, while many consequential developments unfold just out of view.

Unsubscribe https://substack..SPekzIiPMwFsMEmnPKknisUV9B9_TGOj2UlWcBJNY54?
